perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Non-Disclosure Agreement, the term "Confidential Information" shall mean material, information, data and other communications (a) disclosed by NYNEX and/or one or more of its parent, subsidiary or affiliated corporations, appropriately marked as "Confidential," "Proprietary" or the like or otherwise disclosed in a manner consistent with its proprietary and confidential nature; or (b) produced during the course of the working relationship between NYNEX and CONSULTANT, which would either give NYNEX' competitors an advantage or diminish or eliminate NYNEX' advantage over its competitors.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Non-Disclosure Agreement, the term "Confidential Information" shall mean only material, information, data and other communications (a) disclosed by NYNEX and/or one or more of its parent, subsidiary or affiliated corporations, explicitly marked as "Confidential" in bold, red letters; or (b) directly related to trade secrets as determined solely by NYNEX.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked data or trade secrets determined by NYNEX, the contract contradicts the broader legal definition of confidential information, creating ambiguity about what is protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification limits the scope of confidential information, conflicting with UTSA's broader definition of trade secrets that includes information deriving independent economic value from not being generally known, even if not explicitly marked.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract defined confidential information broadly, encompassing material disclosed by NYNEX or produced during the working relationship that would give NYNEX' competitors an advantage. The changed text significantly narrows this definition, limiting it to explicitly marked data or trade secrets as determined solely by NYNEX. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the broader definition of trade secrets under UTSA. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 leads to a 404 error, the Perturbation Explanation explicitly states that the modified definition creates ambiguity about what is protected, and the Law Explanation confirms that the change conflicts with the broader definition of trade secrets. Because the contract reduces the scope of what is considered confidential, and gives NYNEX sole discretion over the confidential nature of trade secrets it contradicts the more general definitions and protections afforded to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has bound its employees, agents and subcontractors to the terms and conditions of this Agreement or that each and every employee, agent or subcontractor has personally executed a Non-Disclosure Agreement containing terms and conditions no less stringent than the terms and conditions contained herein; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of any breach of this provision or the failure of CONSULTANT or its employees, agents or subcontractors to protect such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT warrants and represents that CONSULTANT has informed its employees, agents and subcontractors of the terms and conditions of this Agreement; and furthermore CONSULTANT hereby <PAGE> agrees to indemnify, hold harmless and defend NYNEX from and against any loss, cost, damage, expense or claim arising out of its own direct actions, excluding those of its employees, agents or subcontractors.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'bound' to 'informed' and excluding actions of employees/subcontractors from indemnification, the contract weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting vicarious liability principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law on Agency and Vicarious Liability
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts New York law regarding vicarious liability, where employers can be held liable for the actions of their employees under certain conditions. Limiting indemnification to only Consultant's direct actions may not fully protect NYNEX.
perturbation.justification: The modification shifts responsibility away from the Consultant for the actions of its employees/subcontractors. While no law snippets are provided, the Law Explanation states this contradicts vicarious liability principles. The original contract held the consultant responsible for ensuring its employees/subcontractors were bound by the agreement. The change replaces 'bound' with 'informed' and removes indemnification for actions of employees/subcontractors. This weakens the original protection offered to NYNEX and shifts responsibility, creating a legal contradiction regarding vicarious liability, as the Consultant is no longer responsible for the actions of those working under them, even concerning confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing contained in this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be construed as granting to or conferring on CONSULTANT, expressly or impliedly, any rights, by license or otherwise, to the Confidential Information of NYNEX or any other material, information or data, or any invention, discovery, improvement or product conceived, made or acquired prior to, on or after the date of this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing contained in this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be construed as granting to or conferring on CONSULTANT, expressly, any rights, by license or otherwise, to the Confidential Information of NYNEX or any other material, information or data, or any invention, discovery, improvement or product conceived, made or acquired prior to, on or after the date of this Non-Disclosure Agreement. However, implied rights to use such confidential information may arise through the course of ordinary business dealings.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'implied rights...ordinary business dealings' introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with the explicit denial of rights, weakening the NDA's enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially violates the Parol Evidence Rule, which generally prevents the introduction of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict a written contract. Allowing 'implied rights' contradicts the explicit denial of rights, creating uncertainty in interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: parol evidence rule | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you parol evidence rule Overview In contract disputes, parol evidence is any agreement that is not contained within the written contract. Under the parol evidence rule, these agreements made outside of the contract are inadmissible in court u nless there is evidence of fraud , duress , or a mutual mistake . The rationale behind the rule is to deter untruthful attacks on contracts. The parol evidence rule bars extrinsic evidence , including prior or contemporaneous oral agreements and prior or contemporaneous written agreements, that contradict or create a variation of a term in writing that the parties intended to be completely integrated . In other words, any information leading up to or during a contract that is not included in writing is considered inadmissible evidence and is excluded from the jury . The jury will therefore only look at the writing within the document itself to decide a contract dispute. The parol evidence rule is codified in U.C.C. § 2-202 , which states that terms in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing , usage of trade , or by course of performance ; and by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. One factor the court will look at to determine whether or not to admit extrinsic evidence is whether the written contract reasonably appears to be completely or partially integrated . If the written contract reasonably appears to be, in view of its completeness and specificity, a complete statement of the terms related to the deal, the court will view the written contract as completely integrated. As such, the court will not allow anything outside the writing itself, let alone anything that contradicts the writing. If the contract does not reasonably appear to be, in view of its completeness and specificity, a complete statement of the terms related to the deal, the court can allow consistent, additional terms to supplement the written contract unless those terms contradict the written contract. There are two exceptions to the parol evidence rule: the collateral contract exception and the ambiguity exception . Collateral Contract Exception For the collateral contract exception, the court will look at preliminary evidence to determine if the contract was partially integrated. There are three conditions that must be met in order for the collateral contract exception to apply. The extrinsic agreement must, in form, be a collateral one. This means that the extrinsic agreement must not be distinct and independent from the original written agreement. For instance, the extrinsic agreement could be a side agreement to the original agreement that was made contemporaneously with or during negotiations. And, the same consideration for the original agreement must apply to the extrinsic agreement. The extrinsic agreement must not contradict the express or implied provisions of the written contract. The court gives primacy to writing and does not look at contradictory information. In Baker v. Bailey, 782 P.2d 1286 (Mont. 1989) , the court refused to admit the parol evidence and enforce the extrinsic agreement because the court found that the original written contract was clear and definite and the extrinsic agreement contradicted the original written contract. The extrinsic agreement must be one that the parties would not ordinarily be expected to embody in the writing. The court will apply the “ordinary or natural test,” which asks whether the extrinsic agreement is the sort of promise that one might reasonably expect to be in the original written contract. In Mitchill v. Lath, 247 N.Y. 377,
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'implied rights' which directly contradicts the original text's explicit denial of any rights to the consultant regarding confidential information. This contradicts the Parol Evidence Rule, as explained in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that extrinsic agreements (like implied rights arising from business dealings) are inadmissible if they contradict the written contract. The original contract was intended to prevent any ambiguity of rights, and the modification creates a situation where implied rights could be argued, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT further agrees that it shall not, without the prior written consent of NYNEX, make any news release, public announcement, or denial or confirmation of all or any part of the discussions or negotiations, or in any manner advertise or publish the fact that the parties have entered into discussions or negotiations with each other, or disclose any details connected with such discussions or negotiations to any third party, including any disclosure with respect to this Non-Disclosure Agreement, the negotiations culminating herein, or any phase of any program hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT further agrees that it shall endeavor to avoid, without the prior written consent of NYNEX, making any news release, public announcement, or denial or confirmation of all or any part of the discussions or negotiations, or in any manner advertise or publish the fact that the parties have entered into discussions or negotiations with each other, or disclose any details connected with such discussions or negotiations to any third party, including any disclosure with respect to this Non-Disclosure Agreement, the negotiations culminating herein, or any phase of any program hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'endeavor to avoid' weakens the prohibition against disclosure, creating uncertainty about the consultant's obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Obligation of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly in the performance and enforcement of contracts. 'Endeavor to avoid' is less definitive than 'shall not,' suggesting a lower standard of care, which could be seen as a lack of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text used 'shall not,' creating a definitive prohibition against disclosure. The changed text uses 'endeavor to avoid,' which weakens this prohibition. The Law Explanation suggests this weakening could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that '[e]very contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The change introduces ambiguity and a lower standard of care, potentially contradicting the obligation of good faith as the consultant now has wiggle room to disclose information.
